osaki.alt: 誰かの忘れ物。今日はこれから傘が必要になる天気予報なのに。
osaki.description_text: @ 大崎駅 (Osaki Sta.) 4sq.com/11iLUso (posted via FlickSquare)

Temomin, a chain of mini massage stores, can be found around Tokyo in
many places. This one was inside the station concourse at Osaki.

They just started offering head massages, 15 minute course, while you
lie on a bed. Sort of a "bed head" massage.

The cost is 2,100 yen for the 15 minutes.

What's your favorite massage? Have you tried temomin in Japan? What
chain store massages are in your area of the world?

bakurocho.description_text: Since it was a nice night, we decided to walk from Mominoki House to our next destination in Shinjuku.  The area between the two turned out to be a mixture of low- and medium- density residential/commercial space, which was different from most of the rest of Tokyo we'd been in.  (Most of it was darker than this -- hence no pictures.)

shiodome.description_text: Discovered this bar with amazing views over towards Odaiba. It's located at the top of the Dentsu building in Shinagawa. Dentsu is the great Japanese advertising giant. A trip to it's ADMT (ad museum) is definately worth a visit for those who are into entertaining TV commercials ("CM" in Japanese). The elevator ride up to the top is a treat in itself. It's extremely fast and all glass - makes you feel like you ate riding up in the great glass elevator.

tameike-sanno.attribution: Photo by Kenichi Takahashi on Flickr
Photo by Rob Ketcherside on Flickr
tameike-sanno.description_text: This pedestrian bridge crosses Route 246 right at the remnants of the old Akasaka Lookout (赤坂見附, Akasaka Mitsuke) in Tokyo, Japan. The "Then" photo was taken from the same spot, on a spit of land that led from Edo Castle (behind us and to the right) to the start of the Ooyama Highway. "Now", on the right is the center expressway tunnel entrance and beyond it is Benkei Moat, visible in the "Then" photo. The Outer Moat, visible in the "Then" photo to the left of the spit, has been filled in with buildings and "Outer Moat Road".

When you get to the shrine, you have to climb a serious set of stairs to get to the top. Those stairs put a world of hurt on me.

This is a shinto shrine. Today was also the Sonna Festival, which takes place every only every two years. At 8 in the mornring a parade leaves from the shrine through the streets of Tokyo. We didn't quite know there would be nothing much going on there after the parade left, but we did manage to catch up with it later in the day.
After heading back to the hotel for some rest, we returned to the Hie-Jinja shrine to watch the nighttime festivities of the Sanno Festival.

tameike-sanno.attribution: Photo by lamonse's views on Flickr

tennozu-isle.alt: Shutoko No.1 and Un’yo-maru at Dusk
tennozu-isle.description_text: Taken at a passageway in Ten’nozu-isle Station after sunset. Another photo taken at the same passageway is shown in “Tokyo Monorail and Shutoko No.1 at Ten’nozu-isle.” The white sailing ship beyond the expressway is Un’yo-maru. The fishery training ship built in 1909, and retired in 1929. It was chained in a campus of Tokyo University of Fishery for a long time. In 1962, it was moved to the current place and preserved. The mast of the ship is also shown in “Tokyo University of Marine Science and Technology.”

I deliberately attempted to only use a "film" of 36 images per photowalk. For this set, camera was set to aperture priority mode. I also adjusted the color settings in camera to monochrome / high contrast.

The tozai line to Monzen Nakacho is always crowded.

kikukawa.alt: Barge with Crane on the Onagi River
kikukawa.description_text: When I walk along a river, I see a barge with a crawler crane sometimes, like “Barge with Crane Towed along Jonan-ohashi.” Maybe it is a cost-efficient way to make a floating crane?
Morishita, Tokyo Japan See where this picture was taken. [?] [MAP by ALPSLAB]

kikukawa.alt: Apartment Buildings along the Onagi River
kikukawa.description_text: The tall building is D’Grafort Kiyosumi-shirakawa, and the left building is Plaza Moto-kaga. The building under construction beyond it is Park House Kiyosumi-shirakawa Tower. The construction was completed in 2010 October, ten months after I took this photo.

tsukiji.description_text: Tsukiji Shijo, the largest and busiest fish market in the world, has been a fixture in Tokyo's Chuo Ward since 1935 when the market was moved following the Great Kanto Earthquake. Unfortunately, this is the last year one can see the historic market as it is relocating, controversially, to Toyosu in November (2016). Get there while you can--it's still as vibrant as ever. Also, consider viewing the tuna auctions in which massive specimens fetch ridiculously high prices. Tsukiji Fish Market. Tsukiji, Tokyo.
